data.title: Quest Diagnostics Has Massive Secular Tailwinds And A Strong Base
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Quest Diagnostics (NYSE:DGX) is a provider of medical diagnostic services to markets across the world. Operations are focused on North America, where DGX has more than 2,000 locations. DGX has extensive coverage over the US population with 90% of insured adults in the US having DGX services covered.

DGX is in an advantageous position to experience a secular volume increase from an aging population. While laboratory testing represents under 5% of healthcare spending in the US, it has massive downstream impacts on healthcare spend. Thus, we expect volumes for lab testing to increase faster than the general economy due to both the secular increase in the average age of the population and the introduction of more bespoke testing, such as genetic testing.

We feel that the core business of diagnostic services provides a strong base with a large secular tailwind. More niche offerings such as consumer-initiated testing and the usage of artificial intelligence in diagnosis could also provide longer-term growth. Currently, DGX offers a 2.0% dividend yield and is authorized to repurchase 6.0% of outstanding shares, which, we feel, makes it an attractive addition to an income investor’s portfolio.

Estimated Fair Value

EFV (Estimated Fair Value) = EFY25 EPS (Earnings Per Share) times P/E (Price/EPS)

EFV = E25 EPS X P/E = $9.50 X 18.2 = $172.9

We feel that DGX has a secular tailwind from population aging, which will continue to unlock earnings power as time goes on along with its continued M&A actions. DGX expects 3% organic unit growth, and 1-2% growth from M&A activities, therefore we feel that 18.2x reflects DGX’s superior market position and strong fundamentals.

E2025

E2026

Market Conditions and Results

Routine tests like blood panels represent 90% of testing volumes across the US. These routine tests saw a 13% decrease in total spend, in large part due to decreased COVID-19 testing. Overall volumes for routine testing were down 6% for the same reason. Genetic testing saw 8% spending growth and 5% volume growth.

For DGX, full company revenues saw an overall increase of 2.0% for the first half of 2024. Volume was up 1.1% year over year for the quarter ending June 2024, with 0.7% being organic growth and 0.4% coming from M&A activity. Revenue per acquisition was up 1.6% year over year, due to an increase in higher margin tests being ordered, such as genetic testing. On a more granular level, the diagnostic business, which makes up 97.2% of revenues, saw a 2.3% year over year increase in revenues for the first half of 2024. The service business, which makes up under 3%, was down 6.9% year over year. The fall in the service business is largely due to the decrease in the number of pre-coverage screens for both life and health insurance companies.

In 2014, Congress passed PAMA (Protecting Access to Medicare Act) which sought to consolidate multiple fee schedules into a single coherent fee schedule to bring the rates paid by Medicare more in line with market pricing. Every year, labs must report the rates they charge to private payors, which are then weighted by provider size to determine how much every provider will be reimbursed under Medicare Part B. Importantly, PAMA previously had an upper limit on how much the reimbursement could be cut per year. In 2024, the cap is no longer in place, meaning high-volume tests could rapidly decline in reimbursement level. However, this could prove to be positive for DGX in the longer-run, as PAMA disproportionately impacts smaller labs who are forced to take the low-reimbursement rates. In our view, this could lead to further industry consolidation and either allow DGX to expand into attractive markets at low valuations or effectively limit the scope of competition.

The United States life expectancy has risen steadily since the 1960s, reaching a record 79 years old in 2019. The number of Americans 65 and older will double within the next 40 years to 72 million, while reaching an estimated 80 million in retirement age as soon as 2040. According to DGX, those on Medicare Advantage plans tend to have higher revenue per requisition due to higher reimbursement rates from narrower networks and more tests per requisition. As age ramps up, the per-individual spending on items increases drastically, and thus we feel that DGX has a substantial secular tailwind, even if PAMA pricing pressure continues to mount.

Expansion

The largest M&A transaction of the year was a footprint expansion of Canadian provider LifeLabs, estimated to generate $710 million in additional annual revenues, though it will be slightly dilutive to EPS (earnings per share) for the first year. DGX acquired the provider for $1 billion. Because of the Canadian healthcare system’s more stable reimbursement profile within a population aging faster than the US, and a potential niche for customer-initiated testing in Canada, we expect this business to grow faster than the US.

DGX is also pivoting to a strategy of acquiring or managing in-hospital or in-clinic labs. According to the CEO, there is a general trend of lab requisitions being given to private labs rather than hospital labs, as patients and insurance companies are much more cost conscious than they were 10 years ago. In-hospital labs have large overhead, both due to lower volumes and subsidizing other hospital functions, which cause end-costs to surge by several magnitudes.

M&A activity outside of footprint expansion has focused on diversity of operations, targeting consumer-initiated testing services such as at-home testing kits for common inheritable diseases. The customer-initiated book of business has grown 40% year over year, with half of the growth coming from existing customers, and the other half coming from new customers drawn to new tests introduced during the trailing twelve months.

Another revolutionary tool is AI (artificial intelligence). While most of the media coverage for AI has been on generative AI, the FDA has approved several diagnostic AI models for digital pathology. While patients prefer human-only doctors, pathologists using the technology say it increases their efficiency and accuracy.

In June 2024 DGX completed the partial acquisition of PathAI, using the new acquisition to spearhead DGX’s digital pathology business. According to DGX, pathology is less than 5% digitized. Currently, DGX operates 20 pathology centers where collected samples are shipped and reviewed by a pathologist. In digital pathology, instead of the tech shipping the sample to a pathology center for review, digital pathology takes a scan of the sample and sends it remotely to a pathologist or utilizes AI, allowing more diagnostic certainty and access to better quality care. The digitization of pathology to a single location or remote locations eliminates the need for specialized shipping and gives DGX access to new markets without as much startup or regulatory cost.

Equally, we believe this opens the door for outsourced operations, where a hospital digitizes a sample for DGX to review or DGX conducts the sampling for the hospital’s pathologist to review. This could offer a high-margin business to increase utilization of lab assets. According to Grand View Research, the digital pathology market is still relatively small at around $1 billion, expected to grow at an 8% CAGR to 2030. But the market is still fragmented, and the rise of cheaper computation and AI have accelerated the pace of growth.

Risk

Larger diagnostic providers, like DGX, tend to have lower reimbursement rates with private payors as private payors are given a volume discount which can skew PAMA data significantly. However, as previously discussed, this could offer some advantages for acquiring smaller providers at a discount, provided regulators do not step in. We feel that the removal of the decrease cap will be delayed given its controversial nature and ongoing legal battle.

A hard labor market with limited hirings has impacted DGX. There are two sides to the problem: the employers that are hiring in the service sector are becoming desperate for low-turnover labor and are thus eliminating a drug screen as a condition for employment. The other side is low hiring rates for skilled professions mean less volume during the downcycle. While we feel that once the upcycle starts part of this volume will recover, the trend of eliminating drug testing as a condition for employment is becoming more common.

Financials

Secularly, DGX expects organic long-term growth of 3%, with an additional 1-2% annually from acquisitions. The pace of M&A has led to short-run dilution, but DGX expects Haystack Oncology (acquired in 2023) to be earnings accretive in 2026 with a positive ROI in 2025 and previously discussed LifeLabs to be accretive sometime in 2026.

DGX reported that wage inflation has moderated, with expectations of a 3-4% increase in wage expenses for the full year 2024 which would be about in-line with expected revenue growth. Turnover is still elevated compared to pre-pandemic, though turnover did decrease to 18% for the first half of 2024. Healthcare continues to be a high turnover and high wage growth industry, though we feel some relief will come if inflation continues to moderate. DGX has a cost-cutting program called ‘Invigorate’ which it says will deliver around 3% in annual cost savings through consolidation and productivity improvements. EBITDA margin for the quarter ending June 2024 was 20.4%, which is in line with the long-term average.

M&A activity is largely debt-financed and DGX maintains a positive outlook and BBB rating from Fitch. Currently DGX has a debt to EBITDA of 2.1x for the trailing twelve months ended June 2024, which excludes the LifeLabs acquisition. DGX expects the acquisition to push their debt to EBITDA to just over 3.0x and will target 2.5-3.0x on a go forward basis. We believe that despite the higher leverage target and short-run dilutive nature of some of the acquisitions, the strong base provided by its core diagnostic business will easily provide a runway for deleveraging if required.

Capital priorities continue to put M&A and expansion activity at the top spot, with DGX expecting to spend $420 million in capital expenditure for the full year 2024, which we expect to go toward supporting acquisitions.

DGX expects to generate just shy of $1 billion in free cash flow for the full year 2024, which we expect to end up being at a similar level to 2023, which was $824 million in free cash flow. Currently DGX has a dividend with a 2.02% yield, which is a payout ratio of around 33%. As of the quarter ending June 2024, DGX has a share repurchase authorization totaling 6.0% of outstanding shares: however, we think that repurchases should be put on the back burner in favor of deleveraging after the LifeLabs acquisition.

Conclusion

On top of a strong M&A funnel, DGX has secular tailwinds in the form of an aging population. DGX offers a safe 2.0% dividend yield and is expected to generate just below $1 billion in free cash per year, going toward both maintaining leverage below 3.0x and repurchasing 6.0% of outstanding shares. Altogether, we feel DGX is an attractive dividend buy with some potential for long-term capital appreciation.

data.title: Sweetgreen: Far Beyond The Infinity Kitchen
data.description:
'Fresh' fast-casual concepts set to continue growing in 2025

We all know that Sweetgreen (NYSE:SG) isn’t just about salads. That was made clearer with the introduction of Caramelized Garlic Steak in May. This menu innovation not only increased the average check per guest, but was a key driver of mid-single-digit traffic growth and increased dining occasions in a landscape of guests with tight checks and sensitive to price increases. However, even though it is slowly beginning to branch out its area of ​​activity and become more differentiated through the freshness and authenticity of its ingredients (which is a great driver for premium pricing, by the way), the fast-casual chain founded in 2006 in Washington, D.C. is still widely known for its salads.

Naturally, the environment is ripe for fast-casual restaurants. Fast-food inflation appears to have reached an inflection point in 2022 when price increases reached an unsustainable level. Since then, fast-food restaurants, which had held the highest value scores up until that point, have become the underdogs. Restaurants like McDonald's (MCD) that had increased their prices by more than 100% in the past decade have seen their traffic decline as occasional QSR guests have shifted to c-stores, supermarkets and bakeries, or fast-casual and casual dining.

Now, average fast-food prices were at levels similar to fast-casual entry prices, and for guests who could afford to pay a little more and get a larger portion and better experience, it was the natural path. With this price gap closing, Placer.AI found that fast-casuals saw 3.2% year-over-year traffic growth during the first quarter of 2024, while QSRs saw just 0.4%, virtually flat year-over-year.

One need only look around to see that this is true. While fast-food giants reported mediocre results in the first half of the year, fast-casual chains such as Shake Shack (SHAK), CAVA (CAVA), Wingstop (WING), Chipotle (CMG) and Sweetgreen itself have been showing very consistent performances and beating the LSR SSS Index by a landslide. Others such as Potbelly (PBPB) are approaching QSRs through hook and build initiatives and have succeeded in maintaining flat but consistent traffic from occasional guests, capturing repeat occasions.

Of course, even outperforming the QSR segment, some fast-casuals are struggling, such as Noodles & Company (NDLS) and Yum! Brands’ (YUM) Habit Burger, in addition to some bankruptcy filings such as BurgerFi (OTC:BFICQ), Rubio's Coastal Grill, Tijuana Flats Tex-Mex, Tender Greens (parent company of Tocaya), among others. Thus, even with fast-casuals gaining a portion of the traffic from guests who are fleeing QSRs due to dissonance in the perception of value, this traffic is not indiscriminate, but rather directed to brands that have managed to take advantage of the situation by offering a perception of value that surpasses the rusty value proposition of many fast-food restaurants.

You can be subtle like Shake Shack (in the fast-casual space) and say their burgers are 'Worth It' as in their new campaign, or go all-out like Brinker International's (EAT) Chili's, the famous 'Better Value Than Fast Food' brand. Anything goes when it comes to driving traffic.

Within the fast-casual segment, a specific group of companies has shown substantial growth in comparable sales in recent quarters, standing out for offering salads and other healthy foods. CAVA and Sweetgreen are very interesting case studies for observing this phenomenon. They understood the message from their guests and are putting it into practice with their value proposition. Corroborating this view, Datassential found that around 53% of respondents consider it extremely important for restaurants to serve healthy options, with around 64% of respondents valuing the presence of salads on the menu.

Despite this significant appeal, Technomic found that only 62% (around 310 restaurants) of the 500 largest American restaurant chains offer some type of main course with salad. With fast-casuals generating more appeal on occasions such as dinner, the presence of salads becomes even more necessary. A recent survey by William Blair found that 31% of respondents said they would eat less at Shake Shack due to health/diet reasons. At Chipotle, 8% of the reasons for not returning were for the same reason. These influxes of guests within the fast-casual segment itself are driving growth for concepts that prioritize fresh and healthy food.

As healthy meals are perceived as safe and predictable choices, highly integrated into daily routines, restaurants like CAVA are already implementing hook and build strategies to encourage the return of occasional guests and loyalty of non-occasional. Chief among them is its revamped rewards program, which launched on October 7th and aims to replace the previous one by offering more personalization and surprise gifts for guests on special days.

A growth story that makes even more sense with IK's

Sweetgreen is more than halfway to becoming a company with $1 billion in annual sales, but when it first started, it was just about serving healthier meals to Georgetown University. This seems like a familiar story when we look at successful food ventures.

For example, before its majority stake was acquired by Krispy Kreme (DNUT) in 2018 and later sold to Verlinvest and Mistral Equity Partners for $172.4 million in 2024, Insomnia Cookies was founded by a student who had a simple but brilliant idea. Mr. Berkowitz spent much of his day baking cookies in his dorm room and delivering them to other students at the University of Pennsylvania at night. Another interesting case is Campusfood, a platform that started in 1997 also at the University of Pennsylvania that connects students with local restaurants. Campusfood was eventually sold to GrubHub in September 2011, along with its parent company, Dotmenu. At the time, GrubHub acquired the largest restaurant listing in the country.

After a series of financings and an IPO in 2021, Sweetgreen has retained all of its co-founders (Jonathan Neman as CEO, Nathaniel Ru as CBO, and Nicolas Jammet as CCO) and is expanding its footprint across the country. As of Q2, 2024, the company owns and operates 231 restaurants in 20 states and Washington, D.C. This represents a growth of 12.68% year-on-year and 39.15% over two years. In the first half of 2024, approximately 10 Sweetgreen restaurants opened, 9 fewer than in the first half of 2023.

Given management's reaffirmed guidance for FY 2024, there should be an additional 14 to 16 openings during the second half of the year, with an expected 245 to 247 restaurants by year-end. Seven of those planned second-half openings will be Infinity Kitchen-equipped locations, totaling an additional $3,325,000 in investment for IK. That investment will pay off over time, as new IK-equipped restaurants will deliver 30% margins and 45% lower employee turnover (if efficiencies are in line with the first IK in Naperville, Illinois).

Looking at this data alone, we cannot see the nuances regarding the profitability of a footprint entirely based on IK's in the long term.

To understand the impact of these IKs in practice, I will model the project of two Sweetgreen unit openings. One is part of the 9 openings in the second half of the year that will not have an IK, with the average development costs. The second is a unit with an IK, which means that I will have to add the development costs of an IK (on average $475,000 per unit) to the development costs, but with significantly higher unit-level margins. I will use the same AUVs (since the Naperville unit had similar sales to other Sweetgreen units). Remembering that, I did not calculate an exit price, but rather modeled a perpetual growth rate of 2.5% and used a discount rate of 7.39%.

  • The PV of a unit without IK is approximately $11,405,000. This is almost 30% less than the present value of a unit with IK ($16,122,000);
  • The NPV of a unit without IK is approximately $10,140,000. This also represents a 30% decrease compared to the NPV of a unit with IK ($14,382,000). Note that when we exclude construction costs, the units with IK's end up way ahead, even with the extra $475,000;
  • If the payback time for a unit without IK already reaches the low end of the fast-casual segment (3 years), IK's accelerate to 2 years if the unit can keep its margins and AUVs constant over time. As we are seeing strong entries into untapped markets (we saw strong AUVs in the markets the company entered the last two years, such as Michigan, Wisconsin and Ohio).

It is worth mentioning that Sweetgreen is expected to implement a drive-thru system by 2025 for select locations. This should generate incremental sales per unit and benefit even more units with IK's.

Comparable sales continue to grow in tandem with traffic

As we’ve discussed before, the fast-casual segment is seeing incremental guest traffic with declining value perceptions of QSRs. That said, most fast-casuals have been outperforming the LSR SSS Index lately, and that’s certainly the case for Sweetgreen. See how this plays out in the chart below:

Sweetgreen’s comparable sales peaked after steady year-over-year growth of 4% to 5%. This was driven by increased traffic from new protein-focused menu innovations. The appeal of the new Caramelized Garlic Steak dish also served as a catalyst for transactions for the brand’s other protein-focused dishes, driving incremental sales from non-occasional guests. This has provided a driver for dinnertime sales and should continue to drive traffic as more people associate Sweetgreen with less of a salad-only chain and more of a healthy food chain overall.

Looking at traffic, especially in years where July tends to be weak due to international travel, we can discern traffic trends for Q3 based on how traffic played out in that particular month. Here, we exclude cases where there was a very drastic change in the promotional mix that would eventually generate incremental traffic in subsequent months.

I say this because if January 2024 was the month in which RNA’s RPI (Restaurants Performance Index) fell below 100 (indicating pessimism about current and future industry conditions), July was the lowest point since 2021, when restaurants were trying to adapt to post-pandemic trends or even in late 2010, when the industry was recovering from the GFC. We now know that the RPI in August returned to 98.6, just 0.3 from where we were in June, showing that what we saw was more of a monthly trend than the beginning of a steeper downward trend.

That said, Sweetgreen’s traffic also felt the impact of the Fourth of July, with traffic declining throughout the first week of the month. However, what we did see was a surge in traffic throughout the month, especially in the final weeks, boosting comparable sales by 7% year over year. Of course, this wasn’t just the impact of traffic, but the benefit of bigger checks from the new protein options.

That said, it’s worth noting that Sweetgreen’s traffic has been fairly consistent, especially during Q2 2024. This combined with a policy of constant price increases has led to comparable sales growth at the levels I’ve already shown. Take a look at how this has played out over the past few quarters:

Generating positive operating cash flow is a good start

Looking at how Sweetgreen generates its cash is a good place to start when evaluating its growth strategy. First of all, the company is not leveraged like many restaurant chains (especially fast-growing chains). This is a good sign, since the Financial and Economic Risk is mitigated through the equity-based financing strategy. That is, the company has consistent sources of financing for its operations, with its non-current financing sources properly allocated to ensure liquidity in the short term. Proof of this is the presence of NWC even with a negative net working capital requirement.

This dynamic is observable through the excess of operational financing sources compared to the allocation of capital in operating asset accounts. In Sweetgreen's case, the excess is kept in cash to absorb the impacts of high Capex. However, the cash seems well positioned to face these blows, with the net reductions becoming increasingly insignificant compared to the almost $250 million that Sweetgreen keeps on hand.

With losses becoming less and less impactful, the company appears to be gradually reaching profitable status. Currently, a strategy based on the use of stock-based compensation to maintain a positive operating cash flow. This can be a double-edged sword. While Sweetgreen needs to maintain a healthy operating cash flow to maintain its solid and unleveraged equity position, even making substantial investments in Capex, it needs to balance this with the dilution resulting from this cash preservation strategy.

Now, there are three types of SBC: RSUs, PSUs and Stock Options, all of which are outlined through the '2021 Equity Incentive Plan' (a plan created in the IPO to align the distribution of shares as a reward and Sweetgreen's growth while preserving its operating cash flow). Up to 35,166,753 shares can be distributed under this plan. In the first half of 2024 alone, approximately $20.5 million in stock-based compensation expenses were recorded as operating costs on the income statement, but did not represent a cash outflow per se. In addition to the 2021 plan, Sweetgreen also has an Employee Stock Purchase Plan (ESPP), which allows employees to purchase shares at a discount. About 3 million shares have been allocated to this plan, with the option to increase by 1% each year at Sweetgreen’s discretion. However, in 2024, the company decided not to increase the ESPP.

Since the company made its IPO, the number of shares has grown by almost 3.5%, while only small common stock issuances were made in the same period.

Looking at FCF, we can still see some pressure, but fortunately, it is easing as OCF grows. In 2023, the first year in which Sweetgreen generated positive cash flow, free cash flow was -$63.2 million. This was quite an improvement considering that in FY 2022 the company generated -$140.1 million. In the last twelve months this gap has reduced to -$22.4 million, with Q2 2024 being the smallest gap in the company's history, with an FCF of -$300,000. This means that we are closer to break even than ever.

Valuation

It is actually quite complicated to value a growing company, even more so when it is not yet profitable. Cash flows are still uncertain and the need for reinvestment is quite high. In addition, there are no dividends distributed, and since the company does not generate earnings per share, we cannot rely on the Modigliani-Miller Theory of Dividend Irrelevance to Firm Value.

To keep things a little simpler, we will use the 'Comps' Model and establish a series of comparable fast-casual restaurants to be able to determine the target price for Sweetgreen. That is, by evaluating what multiples the market is pricing these assets at, we will give us some clues about the price of Sweetgreen. I will not lie, fast-casuals are in fashion. With consistently high traffic and healthy check growth, investors will tend to accept more stretched valuations when investing in these companies.

That said, the comparable companies I chose were: Chipotle, CAVA, Potbelly, Shake Shack and Wingstop. The metrics that could be evaluated (given that Sweetgreen still generates negative EBITDA, EBIT and EPS) would be: P/S, P/CF, P/B and EV/S.

Looking first at the P/S, the average multiple for these five companies is 9. With Sweetgreen expecting revenue of $678.42 million for the year (about $5.94 per share, 13.8% RPS growth in FY 2023) we have a PT of $53.46. Now, we can create other scenarios such as an RPS of $6.02 which is the upper end of the estimates. This will generate a PT of $54.18. The lower end (RPS of $5.87) will give us a PT of $52.83. The average of these three scenarios will give us a PT of $53.49, very similar to the expected scenario.

The P/CF gives us a different view. Since Sweetgreen just generated positive cash flow last year for the first time, we still see the valuation quite stretched, even with fast-casuals being valued at gigantic multiples relative to cash flow. With an OCF per share of $0.39, we have a PT of $20.88. That's almost 45% difference from the current share price! Looking at the implied OCF, we notice that for the valuation to be justified by this metric, the company would have to generate operating cash flow of at least $80 million. That's not going to happen any time soon.

Looking at the P/B we notice that the average for comparable companies is 12.7. This means that the PT would be $52.20, since the book value per share is $4.11 for Sweetgreen. If we consider 5% dilution through compensation (as I already warned in the chapter above) we still find a PT of $49.76. Since I believe there will be dilution, but it will not reach 5%, my PT will be $50.98.

Now looking at EV/S, when we take into account Sweetgreen's capital structure, we have a PT of $55.26. This is because the average of the peer group gives us an EV/S ratio of approximately 9.4.

Compiling the results of the 'Comps' Model, we obtain the following situation:

Note that several metrics based on both sales and book value point to the $50 range as the stock's value. I considered it more conservative to average all metrics to find a more realistic target price, since I also based it on cash flow. This gives us a PT of $45.15, an upside of just over 20% based on the stock's current value. Remember that since I am using extrinsic methods to assess my target price for Sweetgreen, this possible undervaluation will depend largely on how fast-casual chain stocks are perceived in the market and less on the cash flows discounted to Sweetgreen's present value.

Yes, this is quite speculative, but when a company is in a growth phase, valuation hovers below the gray area and becomes much more art than science.

My Recommendation

As I said before, growth companies are very difficult to evaluate, but the outlook is quite positive for Sweetgreen. A fast-casual chain that is not only riding the wave of fast-food inflation, but also taking advantage of the growth of healthy restaurants, driven by a Gen Z-friendly approach. Deepening its value proposition and shifting marketing toward protein options seems to drive both traffic and checks. We saw this during Q2 and should see a continuation in Q3.

As for the valuation, we can say that compared to its fast-casual peers, Sweetgreen seems undervalued. That's the most we can say. Perhaps if you are a bit more conservative and like to have a more complete sense of cash flows, Sweetgreen may not be for you. In that case, I would consider taking a look at certain FSRs and QSRs that seem to be less hyped than fast-casuals at the moment.

But we have to admit that they are the ones leading the innovation and transformation of the industry (to see this, just look at Sweetgreen's Infinity Kitchens achieving restaurant-level margins of 31% and Chipotle with initiatives like the 'Cultivate Next' venture fund that brings innovations to the back of the house like Vebu's 'Autocado' or Hypen's automated production line).

However, the thesis of buying Sweetgreen (perhaps a small part of its portfolio) still seems like something that interests me at current price levels. Therefore, I consider Sweetgreen a 'Buy' for now, with a PT of $45.15.

data.title: Is Texas Instruments A Good Buy After The Q3 Results?
data.description:
Introduction

The large-cap semiconductor stock- Texas Instruments (NASDAQ:TXN), noted for its expertise in analog and embedded processing chips, appears to have gotten its mojo back this year. After a disappointing 2023, where it only generated a little over mid-single-digit returns (which represented just a tenth of the overall returns of the Nasdaq for that year), the 2024 performance so far has been quite commendable. Until mid-October, TXN had been outperforming its benchmark but lost ground before its Q3 results which came out a couple of days ago. Nonetheless, the market appears to have liked what TXN delivered in Q3, as the stock gapped, and is up by 4% since the event.

So, how do we feel about TXN after the Q3 event? Well, here’s our read on some of the important sub-plots from that event.

Q3 Earnings - Key Takeaways

Long-standing observers of Texas Instruments would be aware that the business has been facing topline challenges for a while now, and while that has continued for yet another quarter (this trend will only likely reverse from Q1 next year), the pace of decline has abated quite significantly in Q3 with TXN posting single-digit declines (-8%) after a long time (for context, the market was expecting a decline of over -9%.)

On a sequential basis, the topline was up by +9%, but we would advise investors to not jump the gun and expect further sequential progress, as traditionally, on account of seasonal factors, the December (Q4) and March (Q1) quarters usually see a decline in business momentum. Indeed, in the earnings release, management provided rather soft guidance for Q4, with an expected topline range $3.7-$4bn, and an EPS range of $1.07-$1.29. This has already prompted some sharp revisions by the sell-side community with consensus topline numbers coming off by -5% and bottom-line numbers coming off at a much greater pace of over 13%!

The significant EPS revision is due to the pronounced degree of operating deleverage that TXN continues to experience. Operating profits have been falling at a greater pace than the topline since the latter part of 2022, and in recent quarters it has been well over 2x (see image below which does not include the Q3 numbers). Nonetheless, that >2x differential persisted yet again in Q3 with an -18% decline on the operating profit front, vs an -8% decline on the topline). Pressure here will likely persist, as sales in Q4 is expected to drop both annually and sequentially, but yet, TI management expects the OPEX base to be “ flat to slightly up”.

When semiconductor unit shipments are below par, Texas Instrument’s IDM (Integrated Device Manufacturer) characteristics put it at a disadvantage, relative to some of the fabless alternatives that won’t quite see the same pressure on the fixed costs front. The image below provides some context on how far below industry shipments are relative to the normal trend (currently below the 5-year lows).

One may argue that the current slump looks overdone (relative to the long-term trend), but TXN management was not too keen to call a bottom in their largest end-market- the industrial segment (contributes 40% of group revenue), which has been falling over the last 8 quarters, and continued to decline by low-single-digits for the second successive quarter. When your largest end-market is still coping with a bloated inventory position, that reflects poorly on your own days in inventory (DIO) position which is now at decade highs.

Note that after boosting their inventories on the balance sheet by $190m in Q3, TXN is still looking to spend another $100m in Q4 towards this endeavor. With inventory commitments the way they are, it is little wonder that TXN is underperforming its long-term FCF margin target of 25-35%. In Q3, FCF declined by 11% YoY (despite receiving useful investment tax credits to the tune of $220m from the US Chips and Science Act) with the FCF margin coming in only at 9.3%.

Another reason why FCF continues to be hamstrung is because TXN is neck deep in ramping up Phase 1 of their $300m wafer fab investments, which will see CAPEX at an elevated threshold of $5bn p.a. through the end of FY25.

You won’t see the benefits of this any time soon, but in the long-run, TXN will be more competitively positioned from a cost angle, as chips built on 300mm wafers will cost around 40% less than chips built on 200mm wafers and could end up driving gross margin improvements to the tune of 800bps! Needless, to say, as things stand, TXN’s gross margins aren’t in the best shape, and as of 9M-24 stand at only 58.3% (lower by 560bps YoY). In Q3 per se, the gross margin pressure was not as pronounced as in previous quarters with only a 250bps decline (at 59.6%), but management did suggest that GM pressure will continue in Q4 as well.

Then TXN’s top market may still be in a bad way, but its second-largest market (automobiles which contribute 32% of group revenue) did see some green shoots. After declining by mid-single-digits in the first two quarters of this year, growth was finally seen in Q3 at the upper single-digit mark (one could argue this was the main trigger in TXN’s topline coming in better than expected). The key catalyst here was the Chinese part of the auto market where new records are being established. Naysayers may question whether the Chinese momentum could continue, but we’d think this still has some legs as the effect of the Chinese government’s vehicle scrappage program should still linger (Bloomberg NEF believes this scheme could result in EV sales crossing the 10m mark there by the end of this year). Having said that, we would urge investors not to get too carried away, as the Chinese component of TXN’s auto business is only 20%, and management did confirm that the rest of the business has still not bottomed out.

Closing Thoughts- Why TXN Stock Does Not Make For A Good Buy Now

To sum up the previous section, while Texas Instruments did see some green shoots in Q3, abiding weakness still persisted on quite a few metrics (particularly TXN’s largest end market). Crucially, management did not sound confident enough that a bottom was in place. Nonetheless, their cautionary stance is reflected in the Q4 guidance which has prompted the sell-side to cut both topline and bottom line estimates.

In light of this backdrop, we question whether it would be prudent to shed out a premium valuation multiple to own the TXN stock. For context, based on next year’s EPS ($6.1), the stock is now priced at an elevated forward P/E of 33x, which translates to a 28% premium over the stock’s long-term average.

The rationale of paying a P/E premium deserves even further scrutiny when one notes that even two fiscals from now, TXN’s EPS will still be lower than what was seen in FY23.

Then, we acknowledge that a substantial part of TXN’s shareholder base pursues it for its distribution prowess, but as we’ve noted previously, the pace of dividend hikes appears to be slowing in recent years, and the most recent hike has only been around 4.6%. Now, given the share price appreciation seen this year, the yield too is not very compelling, coming in below its 5-year average of 2.63%.

We also don’t think investors should brace for the dividend growth trends to start trending higher any time soon, as this is just a corollary of FCF generation (TXN’s target is to distribute 40-80% of FCF as dividends) Note that, TXN’s FCF generation is expected to stay subdued through 2026 (FCF/share will only catch up with its long-term trend once CAPEX begins to moderate in 2026).

With a relatively unappealing "current" dividend profile (which is of course transitory), we also question why TXN’s relative strength versus other dividend achievers is still on the higher side (43% above its long-term average) and believe it could be vulnerable to further normalization.

data.title: CAVA's Craze Is Justified, But Its Valuation Is Nuts
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Mediterranean-restaurant group chain CAVA Group (NYSE:CAVA) has pulled far apart from the entire group of restaurant chain stocks, both QSR-type (quick-service) stocks such as McDonald's (MCD) and fast-casual dining stocks such as Chipotle (CMG) that CAVA often gets compared to.

CAVA continues to attract a growing share of customers across North America while leaning in on a strategic mix of innovative food menu items and a rapidly expanding store footprint. As the CAVA craze continues on Main Street, Wall Street has continued to revise their growth estimates for the company, now expecting CAVA to grow at almost twice the pace of category leader Chipotle.

The palpitation is real for the company's investors, as CAVA's stock has returned 212% this year alone.

But with such levels of success, I continue to question the rationale behind the sky-high valuations of the company and don't believe this can sustain longer. This is not a case of "Let your winners run" anymore, and the stock is due for a correction despite strong fundamentals.

For now, I am downgrading the stock to a Sell.

CAVA's Craze Looks Justified - But Euphoria Is Elevated

In my previous coverages on CAVA, my skepticism on this name has rested purely on its valuation, which since my last report has stretched even further. The fundamentals for this restaurant chain remain sound, but the valuation is now stretched to levels close to 17x 2024 forward sales, which is too expensive in my opinion.

To remind investors of the company's growth, I will reiterate the last known numbers from the company's Q2 earnings, which showed that the company had posted revenues worth $231 million, up 31% y/y. With these numbers, the company was posting growth rates in excess of 30% on a TTM basis. Gross margins kept the pace as well as gross margins expanding by ~20-30 bp sequentially per quarter. Per the Q2 report, CAVA's GAAP gross margins expanded to 37.5%.

CAVA's rapid innovation in its Mediterranean-based food menu has been one of the key reasons to keep its customers constantly engaged with the brand. In my last coverage, I noted how CAVA's steak debut on the menu was boosting restaurant visits and wallet spend, which investors were loving.

Since then, the company has launched more variations of their popular menu options by adding more flavor to their steak menu with the steak+feta+pita along with new pita chip flavors. On the back of such robust demand, CAVA has also been expanding its restaurant store footprint and has plans to add another 54-57 restaurant locations by the end of the year. That implies a store footprint that should be expanding by ~18% in 2024, which are necessary but lofty goals for the Mediterranean restaurant chain.

External checks show that customer foot traffic and store visits to CAVA's locations stay elevated but have normalized relative to the previous quarter, showing no further increase in monthly visits. In comparison, the fast-casual dining category, in which CAVA is a part, grew at low-mid single-digit rates. The store visits also look to have normalized July to September relative to the strong start to the year.

Therefore, while visits to CAVA stores were impressive and strong, they look to have normalized as compared to the previous quarters.

CAVA's Valuation Multiples Have Only Expanded

But since the previous quarter, CAVA's valuation multiples on all fronts have expanded even more.

Take CAVA's EV/EBITDA, for example, which currently pegs the company at 131x 2024 EBITDA versus category leader Chipotle that trades at 36x 2024 EBITDA. This already points to CAVA being overvalued this year alone.

Investors who might be taking a slightly longer investment horizon might benefit from a 2025 comparison that shows CAVA is still overvalued. Again, assuming Chipotle is appropriately valued for 2025, CAVA looks massively overvalued now.

Chipotle trades at 31x 2025 EBITDA that is expected to grow ~19% next year. In contrast to that, CAVA's EBITDA is expected to grow 28% in 2025, which should warrant a valuation of a 2025 EBITDA multiple of not more than 50x. But CAVA is trading at 103x 2025 EBITDA, implying CAVA will somehow surprise investors by announcing 2025 EBITDA guidance of at least 65% growth in 2025.

Yes, CAVA is growing at impressive run rates, but to expect the restaurant chain to post EBITDA growth rates in excess of 60% is exuberant. For CAVA to post such massive growth rates, the company will have to expand its store footprint at even faster rates and attract customer foot traffic to its stores in excess of 20%, which is unreasonable to expect.

With no visibility of EBITDA growing in excess of 60%, its valuation multiples need to contract to support the growth rates, and I believe CAVA is a Sell for that reason.

Note that the EV/EBITDA valuation multiple has been used through the compare/contrast with Chipotle.

Other Factors To Know

The only factor that can upend my sell thesis on CAVA is if somehow the company can post growth rates that support its 2025 EV/EBITDA valuations of 103x. As I mentioned earlier, customer foot traffic, while impressive, is not trending as much to support the business's required run rates to warrant such a high multiple.

The other surprise factor that may probably keep the company elevated is the whipsaw in volatility due to its high short float. Currently, ~11% of the company's shares outstanding are shorted, which will cause volatility swings.

Takeaway

CAVA remains one of the shining stars of the restaurant industry, somehow managing to post strong double-digit growth rates as an expanding store footprint and constant food innovation keep customers engaged with the Mediterranean restaurant chain brand.

The excitement among restaurant diners has spilled over to investors who have bid up the valuation multiples to 103x 2025 EBITDA. These valuation multiples are completely out of sync with the growth rates the company is posting and are expected to post through the next twelve months.

There is no near-term visibility that points to the company expanding at such a rapid pace to support valuation multiples in excess of 100x, leading me to downgrade CAVA to a Sell.
